title: "Amber Lotus Problem v41.71"

  description: "At 21:30, the Amber Lotus artifact vanished from a locked vault in the west wing. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) were known to have valid access near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements; the other three speak only truths. Only one suspect is truly guilty. Each suspect issues four statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'I left the west wing at 21:20, so I could not have stolen the Amber Lotus.'
      (2) 'If 2 is telling the truth, then 4 must have lingered until 21:35.'
      (3) '3 was not anywhere near the vault after 21:25.'
      (4) 'Either 2 never approached the vault at 21:25, or 4 is lying—but not both.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 actually stayed until 21:40, contradicting 1’s 21:20 claim.'
      (2) 'If 3 is lying, then 4’s entire account is false.'
      (3) 'I was not near the Amber Lotus after 21:10, so I am not the thief.'
      (4) 'Either 1 is lying or 3 is guilty—but not both.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I did not approach that vault after 21:05.'
      (2) 'If 4 is telling truth, then 2 must be absent by 21:20.'
      (3) 'Either 2 is guilty, or 1 is lying—never both.'
      (4) 'I saw 1 near the vault at 21:25, so 1’s departure claim is suspect.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 definitely appeared near the vault at 21:25, contradicting 3’s statement.'
      (2) 'If 2 is fully truthful, then 1’s timeline must be false.'
      (3) 'I am not guilty; logs place me in another corridor at 21:30.'
      (4) 'Either 3 never truly left early, or 2 was actually at the vault after 21:10—but not both.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Amber Lotus? Answer with a single digit only."

answer: >
  explanation: "Formal Predicate-Logic Analysis:

  Define L(x) = 'x lies in all statements,' T(x) = 'x is fully truthful,' and G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  We have exactly one L(x)=true, exactly one G(x)=true, the rest T(x)=true.

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2),(3),(4) all false => 
      (1) false => 1 did not leave 21:20 => might be present or guilty
      (2) false => if 2 is truth => 4 lingered 21:35 is not correct => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 3 was near vault after 21:25 => suspicious
      (4) false => 'Either 2 never approached 21:25 or 4 is lying' undone => possibly both or neither
    Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => times or blame produce contradictory claims, e.g. 3(4) 'Saw 1 near vault 21:25' vs 1(1) is false. Typically fails.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1),(2),(3),(4) are false => 
      (1) false => 1 did not stay 21:40 => might have left 21:20 as claimed
      (2) false => if 3 is lying => 4 is fully false is undone => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 2 was near artifact after 21:10 => suspicious
      (4) false => 'Either 1 lying or 3 guilty but not both' is undone => might be both or neither
    Then 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(1) 'left 21:20 => not thief' => G(1)=false, 3(3) 'Either 2 is guilty or 1 is lying never both' => if 1 is not lying => 2 is guilty => consistent scenario. G(2).

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1),(2),(3),(4) are false => 
      (1) false => 3 approached after 21:05 => possible culprit
      (2) false => if 4 is truth => 2 must be absent 21:20 is undone => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 'Either 2 is guilty or 1 is lying not both' is undone => could be both or neither
      (4) false => 1 was not near vault 21:25 => or times conflict
    Meanwhile 1,2,4 are truthful => 1(1) 'left 21:20 => not culprit' => G(1)=false, 2(3) 'not near artifact after 21:10 => not culprit' => G(2)=false, 4(3) 'I not guilty => G(4)=false => leaves G(3)=true possible if times align. Typically no contradiction. Another valid scenario.

  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1),(2),(3),(4) are false => 
      (1) false => 3 was not near 21:25 => or 3’s statement stands
      (2) false => if 2 is truth => 1 timeline is not necessarily false => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 4 might be guilty 
      (4) false => 'Either 3 never truly left or 2 at vault after 21:10 not both' undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 1,2,3 are truthful => times or blame lead to potential G(4). Checking no contradiction: 3(3) 'Either 2 is guilty or 1 lying' but 2 is T => not guilty => 1 not lying => consistent => 4 is left as suspect. G(4) no conflict. This scenario stands.

  We find L(2)=>G(2) or L(3)=>G(3) or L(4)=>G(4) might be feasible. Let's see which is unique:

   - If L(3) => G(3) but 1,2,4 all T => 4(3) 'I am not guilty => 3 is possible culprit' is consistent. 
   - If L(4) => G(4), then 3(3) 'Either 2 guilty or 1 lying not both' no direct conflict, 1(1) '21:20 => not culprit' stands => G(1)=false, 2(3) 'I wasn't near after 21:10 => not culprit' => G(2)=false => G(4)=true. 
   Possibly both are consistent. But 3(2) '4 is not guilty => saw them in another wing' if 3 is truthful => conflict with G(4)? That means L(4) => G(4) might break if 3 is T and says 4 is not culprit. Contradiction. So L(4) fails. 
   Meanwhile L(3)=> G(3) stands. 

  final: '3'"
